item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not feeling this one too much, it's not bad, but not good either. Maybe if you removed the weird interview and played the weird spa/vacation thing a bit more? I upvoted anyways because the idea is nice and there's a good skip under the mistakes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I suppose what you found could have been very specific to DSM-V as opposed to the DSM-IV. We've probably not yet completely transitioned to exclusive use of the new edition, but I'd say it's at least in widespread use. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
/throws hands in air

Could've sworn I Googled it and it came up as "US is the only country that uses DSM-V widely versus some countries where a few places use it". hrlrllgglglglglglglglslsls

resigned marp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if the Netherlands actually use the DSM-V at all, hence why it's not indicated that they've actually been diagnosed?

We do. Next time, ask the Dutch guy you served with on staff for 3 years. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. :| You might also want to put the intense social anxiety, too. That's another defining trait. Unusual behavior, unusual perceptions, intense social anxiety that does not fade with time.

Schizotypal personality disorder does not have permanent improvements. It's a chronic condition with no cure.

There isn't an actual diagnosis of Schizotypal personality disorder; it's a note that of all the things to compare to, the DSM-V symptomatology is the closest. (I don't know if the Netherlands actually use the DSM-V at all, hence why it's not indicated that they've actually been diagnosed?) Is there a way to better word this so it's clear that these individuals don't actually have Schizotypal personality disorder?

This appears to me to be just a thing which makes people mentally ill and appear in strange ways, and that mental illness is anomalous in a strange way.

The mental illness wasn't intended to be the focus; my initial view of the concept was that it was a variant of niche vacation company that really doesn't understand what humans like and what they don't.

If the hotel aspect was emphasized more, this may be interesting

If there are underlying themes, they should be brought forth harder than they are now, and stitched together better.

Could you give some examples of how to approach this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awkward phrasing:

Currently, of all the survivors4 of SCP-2861's effects, Mr. Jacob Van Dijk (34 years old at time of recovery) has been deemed the most lucid.

Better as "Of all current survivors, Mr Jacob…"

Schizotypal personality disorder

Oh. :| You might also want to put the intense social anxiety, too. That's another defining trait. Unusual behavior, unusual perceptions, intense social anxiety that does not fade with time.

Thus far, no common or Foundation-developed treatments have permanently improved the mental health of SCP-2861 victims.

Schizotypal personality disorder does not have permanent improvements. It's a chronic condition with no cure.

Overall, am I… missing something? People appear in graveyards, some panic, others kind of enjoyed it and develop symptoms of schizotypal personality disorder but are otherwise normal? I am concerned I'm missing the broader implications here. This appears to me to be just a thing which makes people mentally ill and appear in strange ways, and that mental illness is anomalous in a strange way.

If the hotel aspect was emphasized more, this may be interesting, but right now I'm vaguely confused at what the underlying idea really is, or why it's significant. For now it is interesting, but I'm left vaguely confused and unsure what the takeaway is. If there are underlying themes, they should be brought forth harder than they are now, and stitched together better. They seem a little disparate now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, the interview was kind of hard to put together. Leveritas did some double-translation (English to Dutch, Dutch back to English) to try and get a sense of a different speaking pattern, but it might not have been the language best-suited for that approach. Also the overall feel isn't so much gut-punch "dear lord that's terrifying" but more a thin veneer of "something is wrong here" because horror writing is hard :(. Sort of playing off the concept of "the way to tell you have conquered your fear is to arrive at a place where you can welcome it", but that's just a lot of discussion while brainstorming and we did do a fair bit of trimming.

Thanks for the upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "interview log" didn't strike me as particularly believable, but the rest is awesome. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've done something similar with Mandarin Chinese and German, but I guess Dutch doesn't work too well for the double-translation interview approach.

Interview has been edited!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you forget to bar that out in the interview? If not, it seems odd to me to mention the barred out person and not return to them while still having an interview.

It might have been a remnant of an earlier version of the draft mixed in with part of the addenda written afterward. Changes have been made, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant I don't buy that they would just translate the words and leave the sentence structure alone. That completely breaks my suspension of disbelief, because I cannot fathom a situation in which that's an acceptable way to translate something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is Mr. Van Dijk the guy who's name is barred out? Did you forget to bar that out in the interview? If not, it seems odd to me to mention the barred out person and not return to them while still having an interview.

Either way, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And despite being traumatized by the experience, prefer to sleep in circumstances as close to actual burial as possible?

The traumatizing might have caused this instead of working against it, we felt. The people who survived owe that to staying calm, despite awakening trapped with a corpse. That has to leave some mental scars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No one signed up for the anomaly, and there's no guarantee the new preferences are entirely their own.

I'm not sure I buy that, nor the "sentence structure was left unchanged" part of the interview.

Are you familiar with the sentence structure of Dutch? I was hoping to have some grammatical cues that this was a translated interview while still retaining some meaning. Is that impossible here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. It seems like these people signed up for some kind of uber-goth vacation/experience, then were surprised they woke up in coffins? And despite being traumatized by the experience, prefer to sleep in circumstances as close to actual burial as possible? I'm not sure I buy that, nor the "sentence structure was left unchanged" part of the interview. No translator worth their salt is going to sacrifice grammar like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A beautiful trifecta of weird, creepy, and funny that hits all the right notes without overstaying its welcome. +1.

item.title: Re: Credits
item.content:encoded:
Okay, wow, this was a new sort of thing to write. I don't write much creepy stuff, so I guess that manifests in the content of this article.

Anyway, this is the product of some late nights (for me anyway, since timezone differences in this collab!) thinking about what it would be like if someone buried people alive, but with all the best intentions. Also I'm vaguely recalling a terrible vacation I went on with my family. What if being buried alive was considered a spa treatment?

Big thanks to everyone in forums and chat who read over this! :)

item.title: Credits
item.content:encoded:
Co-authors:
Leveritas (original concept, description, addenda, interview double-translation)
Zyn (containment, addenda, clinical tone edits, concept tweaking)

Reviewers
- (chat) AidenEldritch, DrBleep, Rimple, thetataur, Nusquam (x2), PotatoPerson, DrBob, tretter, subtletea, mf99k
- (PM) Bluemorpho
- (forums) Hampo2003, TAL05, jishwa
- Collab inception: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-2113671/i-hope-i-can-work-with-this-idea

Thanks to Zyn, for making this possible.

Edit: Would an image add to the article? I'm curious.
